claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I do! I have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few more questions:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex:

**Do you live with Alex, or if not, do you contribute financially to their upkeep (such as for food, clothes, or pocket money)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me, but I do help out financially. I contribute towards things like clothes, school supplies, and other bits and pieces they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To determine your eligibility, I need to understand more about the financial contributions you're making:

**How much do you contribute towards Alex's upkeep each month (or week), and is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £30 a week towards Alex's upkeep. And no, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. That's very helpful.

Just to clarify one more important detail:

**Are you making these contributions regularly and consistently each week, and have you been doing so for some time?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I make the contributions regularly every week. I've been doing this consistently.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Good News!

**You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

- Alex is 8 years old, which meets the age requirement (under 16)
- You live in the UK
- Although Alex doesn't live with you, you are considered responsible for them because you contribute £30 per week towards their upkeep (for clothes, school supplies, etc.)
- Your contribution of £30 per week exceeds the Child Benefit amount, which satisfies the eligibility requirement
- You make these contributions regularly and consistently
- Nobody else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex

Since only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and no one else is claiming for Alex, you should be able to proceed with your claim.

**Please note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge. You can still claim Child Benefit but may need to pay back some or all of it through your tax return.

Would you like any further information about how to make your claim?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which means they are under 16 and meet the basic age requirement for Child Benefit. While Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant is contributing £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep for things like clothes and school supplies. According to the rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money. The current Child Benefit rate for one child is approximately £25.60 per week (eldest child rate), and the claimant is contributing £30 per week, which exceeds this amount. The claimant lives in the UK, makes regular and consistent weekly contributions, and nobody else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant meets the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old child Alex. Although Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant contributes £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount and satisfies the requirement of being financially responsible for the child. The claimant lives in the UK, makes consistent regular contributions, and no one else is claiming for Alex.
